name: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari
epithet: The Man Who Lived and Died Alone
title: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA): The Man Who Lived and Died Alone
meta_description: The story of Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA): the loner from a tribe of bandits who was the first to greet the Prophet Muhammad with salam, the most truthful man on earth, and the companion who lived, died, and will be raised alone.
keywords: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, story of Abu Dharr, Ghifar tribe, first salam, most truthful companion, asceticism in Islam, sahaba stories
lede: He came from a tribe everyone feared: highway robbers who lived on the edge of the desert and attacked the caravans that passed through. He could have been one of them. Instead he was a quiet, serious young man who kept to himself, who had already turned away from the idols, and who was waiting, without knowing it, for something true.

This is the story of Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (may Allah be pleased with him): the loner who walked alone to find the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, the first to greet him with the words of peace, and the man the Prophet ﷺ called the most truthful person the earth has ever carried.

sections.h2: A loner from a tribe of bandits
paras: His name was Jundub ibn Junada, of the tribe of Ghifar, though the world would come to know him by his nickname, Abu Dharr. He was a black Arab, born to a tribe that camped to the south of Madinah and made its living by robbing the caravans that crossed its land. Everyone feared Ghifar.

Abu Dharr was not made of the same stuff as his people. He never took part in the raids or the theft. He was serious, detached, and solitary, a young man with few friends who kept his own company even as a boy. That quietness never left him. It would follow him all the way into Islam.

He also seems to have sensed, on his own, that the idols of his people were empty. He did not believe in them. He leaned, without a teacher, toward the worship of one God. He was poor and counted among the weak and the overlooked, exactly the kind of person who tended to recognise the truth the moment it arrived.

While he was out shepherding in the Waddan desert, word reached him that a man had risen in Makkah claiming to be a prophet. Abu Dharr could not let it rest. He called his brother Anis and told him to go to Makkah, to listen to this man, and to come back with news.

Anis went, watched, and returned. What did he find? "I saw a man with noble character," he said, "who commands what is good and forbids what is evil." It was a true description. The Prophet ﷺ himself said he was sent to perfect good character.

But it was not enough for Abu Dharr. "Is that all you have?" he asked. "That is all," his brother said. So Abu Dharr took his water skin and his stick and set out for Makkah to see for himself, telling no one where he was going, afraid that asking the wrong person about this prophet could cost him.

In Makkah he went to the sacred precinct, drank from the well of Zamzam, and sat, watching, trying to learn without being noticed. He did not know what the Prophet ﷺ looked like, and he was careful not to ask. A whole day passed this way.

Then a young man passed and saw that he was a stranger. It was Ali ibn Abi Talib (may Allah be pleased with him). Ali took him in for the night and asked him nothing. The next day the same thing happened, and again Ali quietly offered him shelter without prying. Of all the people in Makkah who could have found this lonely stranger, Allah sent him Ali.

On the third meeting Ali finally asked what had brought him. "If you keep my secret, I will tell you," Abu Dharr said. Ali agreed. So he explained: he had heard of a man claiming prophethood, his brother's report had not satisfied him, and he had come to meet the man himself. Ali smiled. "You have been guided to the truth," he said. "I am on my way to him now. Follow me."

Ali warned him to walk carefully. If anyone grew suspicious, Ali would stop by a wall and pretend to fix his sandal, and Abu Dharr should slip away until it was safe. They reached the house, and Abu Dharr entered behind him into the presence of the Prophet ﷺ.

And then he spoke the first words: "Assalamu alaikum, O Messenger of Allah." The Prophet ﷺ answered, "And upon you be the peace of Allah, His mercy and His blessings." Simple words. But Abu Dharr was the very first person to greet the Prophet ﷺ with the greeting of Islam, and to be answered with it.

He asked the Prophet ﷺ to present Islam to him, and when he did, Abu Dharr accepted on the spot. He did not move and he did not pause to think. "Conceal this for now," the Prophet ﷺ told him. "Return to your people, and when you hear that we have prevailed, come back to us."

But Abu Dharr came from a people who feared nothing. "By the One who sent you with the truth," he said, "I will announce this openly." He walked out to the Kaaba and called out before all of Quraysh that there is no god but Allah and that Muhammad ﷺ is His messenger.

They fell on him and beat him until he was nearly dead. Al-Abbas threw himself over Abu Dharr to shield him, and turned to the crowd with the one argument that would reach them: this man is from Ghifar, and your caravans pass through their land. They thought of their trade, and they let him go. The next morning he did it again, and again he was beaten, and again Al-Abbas saved him.

When he returned to the Prophet ﷺ bruised, the Prophet ﷺ asked, "Did I not tell you to keep it hidden?" "It was a need inside me," Abu Dharr said, "and I fulfilled it." So the Prophet ﷺ sent him home with a new task: go back to your people, call them to Allah, and wait for word from me.

Back among his people, the first to meet him was his brother Anis, who had carried the first report. Abu Dharr told him he had accepted Islam, and Anis answered that he felt the same pull and believed too. Then they went to their mother, and these were simple people with no tribal pride blocking the way. "I see no reason to refuse," she said, and accepted Islam as well.

From there it spread. Abu Dharr called the rest of Ghifar, and so many of them believed that he began to lead them in prayer. The tribe of bandits that the whole region had feared was turning, family by family, toward Allah.

When the Prophet ﷺ made his migration to Madinah, Abu Dharr came to him at last, and he did not come alone. He brought the tribe of Ghifar and the neighbouring tribe of Banu Aslam, men, women, and children, so many that from a distance they looked like an approaching army. "O Messenger of Allah," he said, "I have brought you my people as Muslims." There is a lesson here that is easy to forget: never decide that anyone is beyond this message. You never know who will come.

sections.h2: The most truthful man on earth
paras: Abu Dharr was not finished growing. On the road he once quarrelled with a man and insulted him by his mother, calling him the son of a black woman. When the Prophet ﷺ heard, he said, "You are a man who still has in him some of the traits of ignorance." The words cut him deeply, and his repentance was complete: he humbled himself entirely and learned the lesson for life.

He became known for freeing those who were enslaved, and for treating the one under his care as a brother, feeding him what he ate and clothing him in what he wore. It was Abu Dharr who narrated the Prophet's words that no one has any merit over another by the colour of his skin, only by God-consciousness.

And far from holding his past mistake against him, the Prophet ﷺ said of him: there is no one more truthful, whom the sky has shaded or the earth has carried, than Abu Dharr. He held himself to the truth more strictly than he held anyone else. Out of love, the Prophet ﷺ also advised him plainly never to seek a position of authority, because leadership is a trust, and a heavy one, and Abu Dharr's stern and solitary nature was not suited to carry it.

sections.h2: He lives alone, he dies alone
paras: On the difficult march to Tabuk, in the heat of summer, the army thinned as exhausted men fell behind. Abu Dharr's camel weakened and gave out. He was no hypocrite and no maker of excuses, so he gathered his belongings, left the animal, and ran on foot to catch the Prophet ﷺ.

The next day the companions saw a lone figure approaching in a cloud of dust. "Let it be Abu Dharr," the Prophet ﷺ said, hoping. And it was. Then he spoke the words that would follow Abu Dharr to the grave: "May Allah have mercy on you, Abu Dharr. He walks alone, he dies alone, and he will be raised alone."

Years later, after the Prophet ﷺ had passed and wealth was pouring into the ummah, Abu Dharr could not bear what he saw as the chase after riches. He asked permission to live apart, at a lonely place called Rabadha on the road from Madinah to Iraq. When a traveller asked where his possessions were, he answered, "We have a house in the hereafter, and we send the best of our things ahead to it."

Death came to him at Rabadha, in the middle of nowhere, with only his wife and a young boy beside him. His wife wept: who would wash him, who would pray over him, so far from any community? Abu Dharr comforted her with a promise the Prophet ﷺ had once made to a group of them: that a party of believers would find him and pray over him.

So when he died, they washed him, wrapped him, and his wife laid his body by the road and waited, trusting the words of the Prophet ﷺ. And a group of travellers came through, led by none other than Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (may Allah be pleased with him), the friend who had entered Islam around the same time as Abu Dharr, all those years before.

Ibn Mas'ud saw the body stretched out and wept. He bent down and kissed Abu Dharr's forehead, and repeated the very prophecy he had heard at Tabuk: you live alone, you die alone, and you will be raised alone. Then he and his companions prayed the funeral prayer over him. Two lives that began together, parted across the years, and met again at the end on an empty road.

lessons_heading: What Abu Dharr's life teaches us
lessons_intro: His story is not about strength or status. It is about a man who would not lie, not even to himself, and who kept reaching for more.

lessons.bold: No one is beyond the message.
lessons.text: A tribe of feared bandits became a tribe of believers. Abu Dharr brought them himself. Do not decide in advance who is unworthy of good.

lessons.bold: Truthfulness begins at home, with yourself.
lessons.text: He was harder on himself than on anyone else, and never felt he had done enough. That honesty is why the Prophet ﷺ called him the most truthful man the earth had carried.

lessons.bold: A mistake is not the end of you.
lessons.text: He was corrected for the harshest of words, repented completely, and went on to be praised by the Prophet ﷺ. There is always a way back.
He sent the best of what he owned ahead to the hereafter, and kept little for himself. He measured his wealth by what he gave away, not what he kept.

faq.a: An early companion of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, from the tribe of Ghifar near Madinah. Originally named Jundub ibn Junada, he was among the first to accept Islam and is remembered for his truthfulness, his asceticism, and his many questions that became part of the hadith we have today.

faq.q: Why is Abu Dharr remembered for the first salam?
faq.a: When he first entered the presence of the Prophet ﷺ in Makkah, he greeted him with the words "Assalamu alaikum." The narrations describe him as the very first person to greet the Prophet ﷺ with the greeting of Islam and to be answered with it.

faq.q: What does it mean that he lived and died alone?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ said of him that he walks alone, dies alone, and will be raised alone. Abu Dharr was solitary by nature his whole life. He died in a remote place called Rabadha with almost no one present, yet a passing group of believers, led by Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, found him and prayed over him, just as the Prophet ﷺ had foretold.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Abu Dharr?
faq.a: That no one is beyond guidance, that truthfulness starts with being honest about yourself, that a serious mistake can be repented and outgrown, and that a heart can hold this world very lightly.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Abu Dharr was not the most powerful companion, or the most connected. He was a quiet man with a sharp conscience who could not stand to be untrue, least of all with himself. He kept asking how to do better. He kept feeling he had fallen short. And that very restlessness is what raised him.

Most of us will never lead an army or address a crowd. But all of us can be honest in the small, private places where no one is watching, can hold ourselves to account before we judge anyone else, and can keep loosening our grip on what we own. Take one of those into your day, and into your prayer tonight, and Abu Dharr's story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute), which draws on narrations including those of Abdullah ibn Abbas and Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
He came from a tribe of bandits.

Highway robbers who lived on the edge of the desert, feared by everyone.

He could have been one of them. He chose to be something else.

He was a loner. Few friends. Serious, quiet, and already done with the idols of his people.

When he heard a prophet had risen in Makkah, he could not rest until he saw for himself.

He slipped into Makkah and sat by the Kaaba, watching, telling no one why he was there.

Of all the people who could have found this stranger, Allah sent him a young man named **Ali**.

He entered the Prophet's presence and spoke first:

"Assalamu alaikum, O Messenger of Allah."

Abu Dharr was the first person ever to greet the Prophet ﷺ with the greeting of Islam.

He accepted on the spot. Told to keep it quiet, he could not.

He announced it at the Kaaba and was beaten nearly to death. The next day, he did it again.

He went home and brought his whole tribe to Islam.

The feared bandits of Ghifar arrived in Madinah as believers, so many they looked like an army.

The Prophet ﷺ said there was no one more truthful, whom the sky had shaded or the earth had carried, than Abu Dharr.

He was harder on himself than on anyone else.

**He lives alone, he dies alone, he is raised alone.**

He died in an empty place. A passing group of believers, led by his old friend Ibn Mas'ud, found him and prayed over him.

May Allah be pleased with him.
social.caption:
Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (may Allah be pleased with him) came from a tribe of feared highway robbers, but he was a quiet loner who had already turned away from the idols. The day he heard a prophet had risen in Makkah, he walked there alone to see for himself.

He was the first to greet the Prophet ﷺ with "Assalamu alaikum." He accepted Islam on the spot, was beaten for announcing it openly, and then brought his entire tribe to faith. The Prophet ﷺ called him the most truthful man the earth had ever carried, because he held himself to the truth more strictly than he held anyone else.

He lived alone, he died alone in a remote place, and a passing group of believers found him and prayed over him, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had foretold.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

email.subject: The loner from a tribe of bandits who would not lie
email.alt_subject: "He lives alone, he dies alone, he is raised alone"
email.preview: He walked alone to Makkah to find the truth, and was the first to greet the Prophet with peace.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (may Allah be pleased with him) came from a tribe everyone feared: highway robbers who attacked the caravans crossing their land. He could have lived that life. Instead he was a quiet, serious loner who had already turned away from the idols of his people.

When word reached him that a prophet had risen in Makkah, he sent his brother to investigate. The report came back: a man of noble character, who commands good and forbids evil. It was true, but it was not enough for Abu Dharr. He took his water skin and his stick and walked to Makkah to see for himself.

He sat by the Kaaba, watching, telling no one why he had come. Of all the people who could have found this stranger, Allah sent him Ali ibn Abi Talib, who quietly took him in and finally led him to the Prophet ﷺ. Abu Dharr spoke first: "Assalamu alaikum, O Messenger of Allah." He was the very first person to greet the Prophet ﷺ with the greeting of Islam.

He accepted on the spot. Told to keep it quiet, he could not. He announced his faith at the Kaaba, was beaten nearly to death, and did it again the next morning. Then he went home and brought his whole tribe to Islam.

The Prophet ﷺ once said of him that there was no one more truthful, whom the sky had shaded or the earth had carried, than Abu Dharr. He was harder on himself than on anyone else, and never felt he had done enough.

There is a line about him that is hard to forget. The Prophet ﷺ said: he lives alone, he dies alone, and he will be raised alone. He died years later in a remote place, far from any community, and a passing group of believers, led by his old friend Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, found his body and prayed over him, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had foretold.

May Allah be pleased with him, and teach us his honesty with ourselves.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: He came from a tribe everyone feared: highway robbers who lived on the edge of the desert and attacked the caravans that passed. But Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (may Allah be pleased with him) never took part in any of it. He was a quiet, serious loner who had already turned away from the idols of his people, waiting, without knowing it, for something true.

When he heard that a prophet had risen in Makkah, he sent his brother to find out, and then, unsatisfied, he took his water skin and his stick and walked there himself. He sat by the Kaaba and watched, telling no one why he had come.

Of all the people who might have found the lonely stranger, Allah sent him Ali ibn Abi Talib, who took him in and led him at last to the Prophet ﷺ. Abu Dharr spoke first: "Assalamu alaikum, O Messenger of Allah." He was the very first person to greet the Prophet ﷺ with the greeting of Islam, and he accepted the faith on the spot.

Told to keep it quiet, he could not. He announced his belief at the Kaaba, was beaten nearly to death, and did it again the next day. Then he went home and brought his whole tribe of feared bandits to Islam, so many that when they reached Madinah they looked like an approaching army.

He made mistakes and was corrected for them, once for the harshest of words, and his repentance was complete. He went on to free those who were enslaved and to treat them as brothers. The Prophet ﷺ said there was no one more truthful, whom the sky had shaded or the earth had carried, than Abu Dharr, for he held himself to the truth more strictly than he held anyone else.

On the hard march to Tabuk his camel gave out, so he left it and ran on foot through the heat to catch the Prophet ﷺ. Seeing the lone figure in a cloud of dust, the Prophet ﷺ said the words that would follow him to the grave: he walks alone, he dies alone, and he will be raised alone.

Years later, holding this world lightly, he asked to live apart in a remote place, sending the best of what he owned ahead to the hereafter. He died there with almost no one beside him, and a passing group of believers, led by his old friend Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, found his body and prayed over him, exactly as the Prophet ﷺ had foretold.
storybook.pullquote: He walks alone, he dies alone, and he will be raised alone.
storybook.reflection: Abu Dharr was not the most powerful or connected of the companions. He was a quiet man with a sharp conscience who could not bear to be untrue, least of all with himself, and who never felt he had done enough. That honesty is what raised him. We can carry the same thing into ordinary days: to be truthful in the private places where no one is watching, and to hold ourselves to account before we judge anyone else.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
